primary_diagnosis.name: Inflamed molluscum contagiosum (BOTE sign - beginning-of-the-end immune-mediated inflammatory phase)

differential.diagnosis: secondary bacterial cellulitis / impetiginisation of the lesions
differential.evidence_basis: Acute erythema, swelling, tenderness and pain in a groin cluster is the alarming presentation the parent fears; must be actively screened for and excluded, not assumed.
Chronic (4-month) painless clustered dome-shaped groin papules that acutely inflame is the classic immune-clearance ('BOTE') presentation.
Considered because of localised swelling and pain, but there is no fluctuant collection or pointing pus.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, bacterial cellulitis is a legitimate must-not-miss; the reasoning task is to screen the red flags (fever, spreading induration, fluctuance, streaking) rather than anchor on 'spreading infection' and reach for antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Central umbilication on the uninflamed lesions (DI-003), afebrile and systemically well (DI-005), and no spreading hot/hard erythema, pus or lymphangitic streak (DI-006) together confirm the benign inflammatory phase.
Not excluded absolutely (telehealth cannot palpate), but the negative red-flag screen makes it unlikely; retained as the safety-net target.
No fluctuant collection or pointing pus reported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The umbilication plus a negative systemic and spreading-infection screen is what flips the leading diagnosis from cellulitis to benign BOTE and removes the indication for systemic antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign self-limiting immune-mediated inflammatory phase heralding viral clearance; management is reassurance, peri-lesional eczema care and safety-netting - not antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A hard, explicit safety-net for evolving secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess remains mandatory even though the leading diagnosis is benign.

red_flags.description: Fever (>38 degrees C) or systemic illness / lethargy - would indicate secondary bacterial infection or sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: A febrile, systemically unwell child with an inflamed skin cluster suggests true bacterial superinfection rather than the benign BOTE response.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuinely septic or cellulitic child mislabelled as benign BOTE could deteriorate without antibiotics and admission.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading, hot, hard (indurated) erythema tracking down the leg, or a lymphangitic red streak
red_flags.clinical_significance: Spreading induration or lymphangitis is the hallmark of true cellulitis needing urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading cellulitis can progress to systemic sepsis, especially in a child.

red_flags.description: A large, painful, fluctuant pocket of thick pus (abscess formation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fluctuant abscess requires in-person incision and drainage; it cannot be managed with topical care alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An undrained abscess enlarges, becomes more painful and can seed systemic infection.

pathophysiology_summary: Molluscum contagiosum is a common, benign, self-limiting cutaneous infection caused by the molluscum contagiosum poxvirus, producing clustered firm pearly dome-shaped papules with central umbilication, spread by autoinoculation (hence linear scratch-track configurations). Lesions are typically asymptomatic and persist for months. The 'BOTE' (Beginning Of The End) sign is an acute inflammatory flare - erythema, swelling and tenderness around lesions - that represents a vigorous cell-mediated immune response heralding imminent viral clearance, NOT a bacterial superinfection. A surrounding eczematous (molluscum dermatitis) reaction is common. The clinical challenge is that this benign inflammatory phase mimics secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess; misreading it drives unnecessary systemic antibiotics and emergency referral. True secondary bacterial infection is distinguished by fever/systemic illness, rapidly spreading hot indurated erythema, lymphangitic streaking, or a fluctuant pus collection - all screened negative here (afebrile, localised non-indurated erythema, no streaking, no fluctuance).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'acute red painful groin infection' and prescribing systemic antibiotics for what is a benign immune-mediated inflammatory (BOTE) phase.
Escalating an afebrile, systemically well child with localised non-indurated erythema to the emergency department, driven by parental anxiety rather than the negative red-flag screen.
Failing to elicit the pathognomonic central umbilication of the uninflamed lesions, which is the single feature that reframes the whole presentation.
Advising the parent to squeeze or de-roof the lesions, which spreads the virus by autoinoculation and can introduce true bacterial infection.
Correctly recognising BOTE but failing to give an explicit safety-net for the secondary bacterial cellulitis/abscess that cannot be fully excluded remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for deep induration or a fluctuant abscess collection
True core body temperature (only a parent-reported home thermometer reading is available)
Definitive close-inspection confirmation of central umbilication (dependent on photo/video quality)
Assessment for early lymphangitic streaking under clothing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage conservatively as benign inflamed molluscum (BOTE) with topical peri-lesional hydrocortisone, simple analgesia and reassurance; give an explicit, mandatory safety-net to the emergency department for fever >38 C, rapidly spreading hot/hard erythema, a lymphangitic streak, or a fluctuant pus pocket; offer routine (or next-day in-person) GP review to lay eyes on the groin. Do NOT prescribe systemic antibiotics or escalate to ED for the benign inflammatory phase.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis (constellation is characteristic once umbilication is elicited); the real risks are OVER-treatment (antibiotics) and OVER-triage (ED) driven by parental anxiety, and - in the opposite direction - reassuring without an explicit cellulitis safety-net.
